sections.header: Verses 1–5 — “Do Not Be Deaf to My Cry”

heb.paragraph: "To you, LORD, I call; you are my rock (ṣūrî), do not turn a deaf ear to me" (v.1). David cries to God as rock — solid, immovable, reliable. Without God’s response, David is like those going down to the pit (v.1). He lifts his hands toward God’s inner sanctuary (v.2) and asks not to be dragged away with the wicked (vv.3–4).

panels.ctx: A lament that pivots to praise. David cries out (vv.1–2), asks for separation from the wicked (vv.3–4), and declares that God will repay the wicked for their deeds (v.5).

cross.note: "The LORD is my rock" — the same ṣūr imagery.

notes.note: "To you I call, my rock. Hear my cry for mercy as I lift my hands toward your Most Holy Place." Prayer directed physically toward the temple — embodied worship.
"Because they have no regard for the deeds of the LORD, he will tear them down and never build them up." Ignoring God’s work leads to permanent destruction.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

sections.header: Verses 6–9 — “The LORD Is My Strength and My Shield”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD is my strength and my shield (māgēn); my heart trusts in him, and he helps me" (v.7). The pivot: God heard, God helped, David’s heart leaps with joy. The personal becomes national: "save your people and bless your inheritance; be their shepherd and carry them forever" (v.9).

panels.ctx: Thanksgiving erupts: "Praise be to the LORD, for he has heard my cry" (v.6). David’s heart trusts, and he is helped (v.7). The psalm expands: save your people, bless your inheritance, shepherd and carry them forever (v.9).

cross.note: "He tends his flock like a shepherd; he gathers the lambs in his arms" — the shepherd-carrying image.

notes.note: "The LORD is my strength and my shield; my heart trusts in him." Strength (offensive), shield (defensive), trust (relational). The full package.
"Be their shepherd and carry them forever." The psalm ends with the shepherd image from Psalm 23 applied to the entire nation.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVRepay them for their deeds and for their evil work; repay them for what their hands have done and bring back on them what they deserve.ESVGive to them according to their work and according to the evil of their deeds; give to them according to the work of their hands; render them their due reward.NIVHear my cry for mercy as I call to you for help, as I lift up my hands toward your Most Holy Place.ESVHear the voice of my pleas for mercy, when I cry to you for help, when I lift up my hands toward your most holy sanctuary.

rec.text: ""To you I call, my rock. Hear my cry for mercy as I lift my hands toward your Most Holy Place." Prayer directed physically toward the temple — embodied worship."

rows.label: The Cry — 28:1–5
rows.text: "My rock, do not be deaf." Hands lifted. Separation from the wicked.

hebtext.note: "To you, LORD, I call; you are my rock (ṣūrî), do not turn a deaf ear to me" (v.1). David cries to God as rock — solid, immovable, reliable. Without God’s response, David is like those going down to the pit (v.1). He lifts his hands toward God’s inner sanctuary (v.2) and asks not to be dragged away with the wicked (vv.3–4).

"The LORD is my strength and my shield (māgēn); my heart trusts in him, and he helps me" (v.7). The pivot: God heard, God helped, David’s heart leaps with joy. The personal becomes national: "save your people and bless your inheritance; be their shepherd and carry them forever" (v.9).

thread.text: "The LORD is my rock" — the same ṣūr imagery.
"He tends his flock like a shepherd; he gathers the lambs in his arms" — the shepherd-carrying image.

themes.note: Faith: trust produces help. Sovereignty: God is rock, strength, shield. From personal cry to national prayer.
